about.body: Podable is an app created to provide objective metrics to players for rating their decks by tracking statistics from played games.

Match-making is a real problem for Magic: the Gathering players. Often times, experienced players are unaware of how their decks perform against others, leading to one-sided games against their opponents.

Podable attempts to solve this problem by keeping records of win rates for each commander. This information can be used to determine the average strength of a deck, allowing players to make more informed decisions when choosing which decks to play with.

Podable is built and maintained by:
 
 
©️ 2025

faq.body: Pods are a group of people who are playing or have played a game of Magic: the Gathering together. The term is almost exclusively used when refering to the Commander format, which has 4 players rather than the standard 2 players.
Podable is an app which keeps track of game state properties, such as player life and damage totals.
Podable can track the Commanders played, the player life totals, number of turns in a game, the time span of each turn, and the winning Commander.

Currently, there is a lack of hard data for gauging the way Commander games unfold. Important game state values are logged in order to build robust statistics for how each commander is played. This data has the potential to allow players to make more informed decisions about the games they wish to play.
For example: by knowing the average game duration for their commanders, players can choose a deck that finishes games within a certain time frame.

rules.body: Rule Zero is the concept for which players could bring forward specific rules violations within their deck prior to starting play and the other players would decide whether to accept and play against the deck or not.  Rule zero has mostly been replaced with the pregame discussion about deck brackets.

rules.subtitle: It's not if you win or lose, its how you play the game.

rules.body: The thrill of winning can be exhilerating, however, be mindful that with every winner, there are three other people who lost. Refrain from gloating over your plays and reiterating how the others were defeated. Instead, make sure to complement your opponents on their unique strategies and how they piloted their deck. Strive to be the player who makes the game fun for everyone.

rules.subtitle: The game ends with a handshake

rules.body: Once a winner has been declared, it is the duty of the defeated to offer the handshake to the winner. This symbolizes the acknowledgment of loss. Those who practice this jesture are regarded as admirable players withiin the community. If there is no handshake at the end, the game is said to be contentious, where some players disagree about the winning state. It is best to resolve this issue before continuing to the next round.

brackets.body: Wizards of the Coast (WotC) recently released a bracket system for Magic: The Gathering Commander players to assign their decks into. The goal is to provide a categorical framework for deck selection based on various card power levels and to encourage players to start on equal footing with each other.

What WotC has failed to communicate is that the issue of mismatched deck power levels isn't about the decks themselves, but rather about the intent of the people playing them. Magic: The Gathering players have various reasons for including certain cards – ranging from exploring new combos to reusing powerful cards in hopes of sneaking in a win. Here, we discuss how player intent falls into each bracket.

Magic: The Gathering is a game of fantasy worlds with extensive backstories. Bracket 1 focuses on thematic deck world-building. Players are incentivized to weave a coherent story with each card they play. This bracket is commonly misinterpreted as being for weak cards and weak decks. This is untrue. Powerful decks can be built from thematic card sets – it just takes creativity to unlock that power. In Bracket 1, players focus on sharing an experience rather than trying to win at all costs.

Bracket 2 is for players who wish to test out their deck-building and piloting skills. Decks in this bracket have typically gone through several revisions since their last game, making players eager to get their deck to "do the thing." If everyone in the pod gets a chance to do the thing, the game is considered a success.

Modified preconstructed decks form the basis of Bracket 3. The term "Precon" (preconstructed) has been redefined to better reflect how players use it in practice.
"Precon": A pre-game con pulled on opponents regarding a deck's power level, where a player tries to convince the table that the deck they purchased is weaker than it actually is – usually by claiming they only swapped out <number> of cards.
Bracket 3 is where players begin to one-up each other, allowing for varied gameplay and strategy. Game-changing cards can be included in the deck, but are not necessary.

Bracket 4 is for players who haven't yet realized that dying in game doesn't mean they die in real life. Players in this bracket often build decks that prioritize frustrating, uninspiring, and/or repetitive play patterns. Imposing mental anguish on their friends is often a goal for Bracket 4 players. These decks are highly tuned and packed with powerful game-changing cards. They're often predictable – knowing the commander is usually enough to guess the strategy.

Bracket 5 focuses on the most strategy of all: winning. This bracket is more commonly known as cEDH (Competitive Commander). Here, players homogenize their decks to cheat out a win as fast as possible using the most broken cards. Since many of the best cards come from an era when Magic's designers weren’t thinking about multiplayer, they’re often extremely expensive. As a result, players fill their decks with proxy (counterfeit) cards and pretend they’re the real deal. Games typically revolve around politics and deal-making. Very little variation exists in deck construction within this bracket.
Highly doubtful – but maybe. That's why Podable keeps track of the bracket ratings that players give to their decks.

brackets.subtitle: How are Brackets represented in Podable?

brackets.body: Players are given the chance to assign a bracket to their deck during pregame setup. When a player wins a game, the bracket they chose becomes the ground truth. However, opponents can also rate each other's decks. This secondary metric helps players gauge whether their rating was fair. If a player notices that opponents consistently rate their deck differently, it may be worth reflecting on why – and possibly choosing a different bracket next time.

analytics.subtitle: Unlock the Hidden Patterns in Your Games

analytics.body: Podable's advanced analytics transform raw game data into actionable strategic insights. Each visualization reveals different aspects of your gameplay, helping you understand not just what happened, but why it happened and how to improve.

analytics.subtitle: See the Story of Every Game

analytics.body: The Game Flow Heat Map displays turn-by-turn intensity for each player, revealing when games are won and lost. Each cell represents one turn, colored by its 'impact level' on the game outcome.

Turn intensity combines multiple factors:
• Turn Duration (longer turns often indicate complex, impactful plays)
• Commander Damage Dealt (aggressive turns that pressure opponents)
• Game Position (late-game turns carry more weight)
• Outcome Influence (turns that led to wins are highlighted)

🟢 Green = Low intensity (setup, passive turns)
🟡 Yellow = Medium intensity (meaningful plays)
🔴 Red = High intensity (game-changing moments)
🔵 Blue border = Your active turn
👑 Gold = Winning turn
❌ = Elimination turn

• Identify when you typically become threatening (and when others do)
• Spot patterns in successful vs failed games
• Time your interaction and threats more effectively
• Recognize when to hold back vs when to commit resources
Example: If your wins always show 2-3 consecutive red cells before victory, you know you need to 'chain together' impactful turns rather than making isolated big plays.
The Ecosystem Map reveals relationships between commanders in your playgroup, showing frequency pairings, head-to-head matchups, and meta health metrics.

• Node Size = How often a commander is played
• Node Color = Win rate performance
• Connections = Games played together
• Connection Thickness = Pairing frequency
This color-coded grid shows win percentages when specific commanders face each other. Darker colors indicate higher win rates in that matchup.

Diversity Score: Measures how evenly commanders are played (higher = more variety)
Balance Score: Measures win rate distribution (higher = more competitive balance)
Your meta is healthiest when both scores are above 70%. Low diversity suggests commander stagnation; low balance indicates power level mismatches.

• Identify favorable and unfavorable matchups for your commanders
• Discover underplayed commanders that might surprise opponents
• Recognize meta trends and adapt accordingly
• Find commanders that pair well together in multiplayer

analytics.subtitle: Deep Dive Into What Makes Decks Successful

analytics.body: Enhanced performance views go beyond simple win rates to analyze efficiency, consistency, and situational performance.

Combines win rate with average turn speed: Efficiency = (Win Rate × Turn Speed Factor)
Faster decision-making with good results indicates mastery of a deck.

• Standard Deviation of Game Lengths (lower = more consistent)
• Turn Pattern Reliability (do you follow similar play sequences?)
• Bracket Accuracy (does your power level match your rating?)

• Performance by Turn Order (do you play better going first/last?)
• Pod Size Impact (how do you perform in 3vs4 player games?)
• Time-of-Day Effects (are you sharper during certain hours?)

analytics.subtitle: Master the Rhythm of Your Gameplay

analytics.body: Turn analysis reveals the pacing and patterns that separate good players from great ones.

Each commander shows average turn length by turn number. This reveals:
• Setup Phase Length (how many turns until you're active)
• Decision Complexity (when your turns become most complex)
• Endgame Efficiency (do you close out games quickly?)

Successful players often show consistent turn rhythms:
• Fast early turns (quick setup decisions)
• Longer mid-game turns (complex interactions)
• Fast late-game turns (decisive action when ahead)

• If your early turns are long: streamline your setup decisions
• If your late turns are long: practice recognizing win conditions
• If patterns are erratic: work on strategic planning
Bracket analysis helps ensure fair, fun games by tracking power level accuracy and suggesting adjustments.

Compares your bracket self-ratings with actual performance:
• Over-rating: Lower win rate than expected for bracket
• Under-rating: Higher win rate than expected for bracket
• Accurate: Performance matches bracket expectations

When opponents rate your deck differently than you do, it suggests:
• Perception Gap: Your deck feels stronger/weaker than you think
• Pilot Effect: Your skill level affects the deck's actual power
• Meta Context: Power is relative to your specific playgroup

The app analyzes your performance trends and suggests bracket adjustments:
• Consistent over-performance → Consider moving up a bracket
• Consistent under-performance → Consider moving down
• High variance → Deck power may be inconsistent

faq.body: Pods are a group of people who are playing or have played a game of Magic: the Gathering together. The term is almost exclusively used when refering to the Commander format, which has 4 players rather than the standard 2 players.
Podable is an advanced game tracker that logs game state properties and transforms them into actionable strategic insights through revolutionary visualizations and analytics.

Podable tracks Commanders played, life totals, commander damage, poison counters, turn durations, game outcomes, and much more. But more importantly, it analyzes this data to reveal patterns and provide strategic insights.

Traditional tracking tells you what happened. Podable's analytics tell you WHY it happened and HOW to improve. The Game Flow Heat Map, for example, shows exactly when games are won and lost, helping you time your plays perfectly.
Insights improve with more data. With 5+ games per commander, basic patterns emerge. With 20+ games, the analytics become highly reliable for strategic planning.

faq.title: Do I need to understand the math?

faq.body: Not at all! The visualizations are designed to be intuitive. The Game Flow Heat Map looks like a game board, the Ecosystem Map shows relationships visually, and all insights come with plain-English explanations.

faq.title: How do I improve using these tools?

faq.body: Start with the Game Flow Heat Map to identify your turn patterns. Look for differences between wins and losses. Then use the Ecosystem Map to understand favorable matchups. Finally, check Performance Analytics for specific improvement areas.
For example: if your heat maps show you're always eliminated during high-intensity periods, you need better defensive options or threat assessment skills.

rules.body: Rule Zero is the concept for which players could bring forward specific rules violations within their deck prior to starting play and the other players would decide whether to accept and play against the deck or not.  Rule zero has mostly been replaced with the pregame discussion about deck brackets.

rules.subtitle: It's not if you win or lose, its how you play the game.

rules.body: The thrill of winning can be exhilerating, however, be mindful that with every winner, there are three other people who lost. Refrain from gloating over your plays and reiterating how the others were defeated. Instead, make sure to complement your opponents on their unique strategies and how they piloted their deck. Strive to be the player who makes the game fun for everyone.

rules.subtitle: The game ends with a handshake

rules.body: Once a winner has been declared, it is the duty of the defeated to offer the handshake to the winner. This symbolizes the acknowledgment of loss. Those who practice this jesture are regarded as admirable players withiin the community. If there is no handshake at the end, the game is said to be contentious, where some players disagree about the winning state. It is best to resolve this issue before continuing to the next round.

While Podable's analytics can give you competitive advantages, remember that the goal is fun, fair games. Use insights to improve your play and build better decks, not to dominate casual tables. Share interesting patterns with your playgroup - everyone benefits when the meta becomes more balanced and engaging.

Bracket 3 is where players begin to one-up each other, allowing for varied gameplay and strategy. Game-changing cards can be included in the deck, but are not necessary.

Here, players homogenize their decks to cheat out a win as fast as possible using the most broken cards. As a result, players fill their decks with proxy (counterfeit) cards and pretend they're the real deal. Games typically revolve around politics and deal-making. Very little variation exists in deck construction within this bracket.

Podable's Bracket Analysis goes beyond simple self-rating. It tracks:
• Performance vs Expected (are you over/under-rating?)
• Opponent Feedback (how others perceive your deck's power)
• Dynamic Suggestions (recommended bracket adjustments based on data)

The Ecosystem Map shows bracket distribution in your meta. Healthy metas show:
• Even spread across 2-3 brackets (variety without extreme gaps)
• Low variance in win rates within brackets (balance)
• Players accurately rating their decks (self-awareness)

Highly doubtful – but maybe. That's why Podable keeps track of bracket ratings and analyzes their accuracy. The real solution might be better communication and data-driven insights about actual deck performance.

brackets.subtitle: How are Brackets represented in Podable?

brackets.body:
Players assign brackets during pregame setup. The app tracks:
• Your self-ratings over time
• Opponent ratings of your decks
• Performance correlation with ratings
• Suggested adjustments based on data

This creates a feedback loop that improves bracket accuracy and leads to more balanced, enjoyable games.
